詹姆斯·弗雷德里克·布里登斯廷(James Frederick Bridenstine)担任NASA局长30个月多一点，他首先要知道的是，无论总统选举结果如何，他都不会继续担任航天局局长。
并不是说他想辞去这份工作。布里登斯廷一直很享受领导NASA度过动荡时期的挑战，并克服了最初对他的党派偏见的担忧，领导NASA-所有NASA-度过特朗普政府的动荡岁月。这也不是因为他失败了。布里登斯廷在很大程度上成功地推动了该机构的发展，并将让它比他发现的更好。
但现实是，民主党总统永远不会让布里登斯汀继续担任行政长官，她有政治背景，而不是技术背景。他知道这一点。他本周也说了同样的话，他告诉航空周刊，新总统可能会想要另一个人，一个完全信任的人。毕竟，他之前曾提出立法，将地球科学从NASA的使命声明中删除，他还批评了同性婚姻。布里登斯汀将于1月20日辞职。
然而，他也不会回来迎接第二个特朗普政府。在2018年4月开始的NASA局长任期内，布里登斯廷信奉气候科学，支持地球科学任务。此外，总统的顾问希望布里登斯汀更多地抨击他的前任，将特朗普太空计划的成功与奥巴马总统的失败进行对比。但布里登斯汀或多或少坚持了立场，认为是他的前任创造并资助了商业机组项目，导致了SpaceX在5月份戏剧性的载人飞行。
历史学家约翰·洛格斯顿(John Logsdon)说，他一直是美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的管理员，而不是特朗普在NASA的代表。自1958年NASA成立以来，他就认识该机构的所有管理员。
多个消息来源证实，如果特朗普再次当选，布里登斯汀可能会下台或被赶下台。他这样做有合法的家庭原因--这位45岁的男子有一个年轻的、不断壮大的家庭，他渴望在俄克拉何马州花更多的时间和他们在一起。但也有明确的信号表明，第二届特朗普政府将把不关心政治的NASA变成一个更具政治性的机构。
例如，在接下来的几个月里，NASA计划举行一场精心设计的仪式，以隐藏人物玛丽·杰克逊(Mary Jackson)的名字正式重新命名NASA总部。这场活动的主角是总统的女儿伊万卡·特朗普(Ivanka Trump)。沿着这些思路，NASA也会有一个新的领导人--与其说是NASA的行政人员，不如说是该航天局的特朗普代表。
在一次采访中，Logsdon说他评价布里登斯汀在NASA的任期是成功的。这位历史学家和总统太空政治专家表示：我认为他在这一职位上的表现超出了任何人的预期。
Logsdon列举了两个主要的成功案例。他说，其一是布里登斯汀稳定了该机构的项目。特别是，通过阿耳特弥斯计划，布里登斯廷已经为一项将人类送回月球并最终送上火星的计划获得了两党的支持。他还在许多行业内获得了对这一想法的支持，并开始让国际合作伙伴加入到重要承诺的行列中来。
布里登斯汀这样做的同时，也控制了人们对阿特米斯是一个政治计划的看法，将人类登上月球的目标日期定在2024年--这将是特朗普第二个任期的最后一年。洛格斯登说，他认为有理由期待阿耳特弥斯在当选总统乔·拜登(Joe Biden)的领导下以某种形式继续存在，尽管首次登月不太可能在本世纪20年代下半叶之前实现。
洛格斯登还称赞布里登斯汀认识到了太空时代的变化-以SpaceX为首的商业公司正在为探索贡献更多的私人资金和想法-并欣然接受了这些变化。他领导了从旧NASA到新NASA的过渡，特别是强调公私合作以及美国私营部门的参与，这一点比他的任何一位前任都要强烈。
当然，布里登斯汀并不完美。该机构内部人类探索之外的领域有时感觉在很大程度上被布里登斯汀忽视了。宇航员办公室的一些人也因为出现在白宫和其他有利于特朗普政府的活动而感到政治化。布里登斯汀也得到了帮助：迈克·彭斯(Mike Pence)的一位支持她的副总统，以及斯科特·佩斯(Scott Pace)领导的国家太空委员会(National。但布里登斯汀是美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的公众形象，带头冲锋。
毫无疑问，布里登斯汀和他的团队一直在努力改进NASA，使其走上可持续发展的道路。
主引擎切断播客的创始人安东尼·科兰杰洛(Anthony Colangelo)说，他刚刚推出了《美国太空复兴法案》(American Space Renaissance Act)，这是一部汇集了大量太空政策思想的法案。总的来说，这听起来像是对太空爱好者过去几年来一直在讨论、辩论和徘徊的所有想法的整理。看到这些前瞻性的政策想法被加入到国会的混杂之中，真的让人们兴奋不已。
布里登斯汀对太空的真正热情也帮助赢得了像科兰杰洛这样的太空爱好者和观察家的支持。布里登斯汀会像粉丝一样热情地谈论这些话题。他在国会听证会上喝了山露酒。科兰杰洛说：听起来他好像本可以就在我们身边，在Twitter、Reddit、NASASpaceFlight论坛或你最喜欢的播客上谈论和争论太空问题。
在那些已经关心太空的人中，这种热情是有感染力的。真正的问题是，这种对近地轨道以外的探索的渴望能否延伸到太空社区之外。对此最好的考验是，国会最终是否会资助阿耳特弥斯计划(Artemis Program)。NASA在2021财年预算中为人类着陆系统寻求了超过30亿美元的资金，但现在看来国会将提供6亿至10亿美元。尽管这要少得多，但它仍可能被视为为月球计划提供资金的基线承诺，尽管时间表较慢。
最终，吉姆·布里登斯汀的遗产很可能取决于这些资金是暂时的，还是最终真的会让第一个女人和下一个男人穿着NASA的宇航服登上月球。
note_en:
The first thing to know about James Frederick Bridenstine, who has served as NASA's administrator for a little more than 30 months, is that he was not staying on as the space agency's leader regardless of the presidential election results.
 Not that he wants out of the job. Bridenstine has relished the challenge of leading NASA through troubling times and overcoming initial concerns about his partisanship to lead NASA— all of NASA—through the turbulent years of the Trump administration. Nor is it because he has failed. Bridenstine has largely succeeded in pushing the agency forward and will leave it better than he found it.
 But the reality is that a Democratic president was never going to keep Bridenstine, who has a political rather than a technical background, on as administrator. And he knew this. He said as much this week,  telling  Aviation Week that a new president would probably want someone else, someone fully trusted. After all, he had previously  introduced legislation to remove Earth science from NASA's mission statement, and he criticized  same-sex marriages. Bridenstine will resign his position on January 20.
 However, he would not have come back for a second Trump administration, either. During his tenure as NASA administrator, which began in April, 2018, Bridenstine embraced climate science and supported Earth science missions. Moreover, the president's advisors wanted Bridenstine to bash his predecessors more, to contrast the "success" of the Trump space program with the "failure" of President Obama's. But Bridenstine more or less held the line, crediting his predecessors for creating and funding the commercial crew program that led to SpaceX's dramatic  crewed flight in May.
  "He has been a NASA administrator, not a Trump representative at NASA," said John Logsdon, a historian who has known all of the agency's administrators since its inception in 1958.
 Multiple sources have confirmed that Bridenstine would have stepped down or been moved aside had Trump been reelected. He had legitimate family reasons for doing so—the 45-year-old has a young and growing family and desire to spend more time with them in Oklahoma. But there were also clear signals that the a second Trump administration would have turned the apolitical NASA into a more political agency.
 For example, within the coming months, the agency planned to hold an elaborate ceremony to formally rename the NASA Headquarters after "Hidden Figure" Mary Jackson. The event was to feature Ivanka Trump, the president's daughter. Along these lines, NASA would get a new leader as well—not so much a NASA administrator, but a Trump representative at the space agency.
  In an interview, Logsdon said he rated Bridenstine's term at NASA a success. " I think he’s exceeded anyone’s expectations in the position," the historian and expert in presidential space politics said.
 Logsdon cited two primary successes. One, he said, is that Bridenstine stabilized the agency's programs. In particular, with the Artemis Program, Bridenstine has built bipartisan support for a plan to send humans back to the Moon and eventually on to Mars. He has also engendered support within much of industry for this idea and  began to bring international partners on board with important commitments.
  Bridenstine also did this while managing perceptions that Artemis was a "political" program, with a convenient target date of 2024 for landing humans on the Moon—what would have been the final year of a second Trump term. Logsdon said he believes it is reasonable to expect that Artemis will continue in some form under President-elect Joe Biden's administration, although the first Moon landing is unlikely before the second half of the 2020s.
 Logsdon also credited Bridenstine with recognizing the changing times in space—commercial companies, led by SpaceX, are contributing more private money and ideas to exploration—and embraced them. " He’s led the transition from old NASA to new NASA, in particular with the emphasis on public-private partnerships, and the engagement of the US private sector, more strongly than any of his predecessors."
 Bridenstine has not been perfect, of course. Areas outside of human exploration within the agency have at times felt largely ignored by Bridenstine. Some in the astronaut office, too, have felt politicized by their appearances at the White House and other events for the benefit of the Trump administration. Bridenstine also had help: A supportive vice president in Mike Pence and a National Space Council led by Scott Pace. But Bridenstine was the public face of NASA, leading the charge.
  There can be little doubt that Bridenstine and his team have sought to improve NASA and put it on a sustainable course.
 "He came into the conversation having just rolled out the American Space Renaissance Act, which was a huge collection of thoughts on space policy," said Anthony Colangelo, founder of the Main Engine Cutoff Podcast. "It generally sounded like a collation of all the ideas that space enthusiasts had been discussing and debating and circling around for the past few years. To see those forward-looking policy ideas thrown into the Congressional mix really got people excited."
 Bridenstine's genuine enthusiasm for space also helped win over space fans and observers like Colangelo. Bridenstine would talk about these topics with the same passion as fans. He drank Mountain Dew at Congressional hearings. "He sounded like he could have been right alongside us talking and arguing about space issues on Twitter or Reddit or NASASpaceflight forums or on your favorite podcast," Colangelo said.
 Among people who already care about space, this enthusiasm was infectious. The real question is whether this desire for exploration beyond low-Earth orbit can be extended beyond the space community. The best test of this is whether Congress ultimately funds the Artemis Program. NASA sought more than $3 billion for a Human Landing System in the fiscal year 2021 budget, but it now looks like Congress will provide $600 million to $1 billion. Although this is considerably less, it might still be seen as a baseline commitment to funding the lunar program, albeit on a slower timeline.
 Ultimately, Jim Bridenstine's legacy will probably depend on whether such funding proves transitory or ultimately does in fact lead to the first woman and the next man landing on the Moon in NASA spacesuits.
title: 吉姆·布里登斯汀即将离开美国国家航空航天局。我们应该如何评价他30个月的任期呢？
title_en: Jim Bridenstine is leaving NASA. How should we assess his 30-month tenure?

note:
本田声称，它将是第一家批量生产具有自动驾驶能力的汽车的汽车制造商，这些汽车符合SAE Level 3标准，并计划从明年3月开始在日本生产和销售配备完全经过批准的自动驾驶设备的本田传奇(Honda Legend)豪华轿车。本田通过新闻稿(通过路透社)宣布了这一消息，此前该公司的“交通堵塞飞行员”自动驾驶技术获得了日本政府的批准，该技术将首次允许司机在忙碌时将视线从道路上移开。
本田(Honda)的Pro Pilot Assistate功能早于这款即将推出的功能，但根据SAE标准，这是一个二级功能，这意味着虽然它可以自动控制速度和转向，但方向盘后面的司机必须时刻准备好在系统需要时接管手动控制。SAE 3级是第一个属于大多数专家认为有资格被归类为实际自动驾驶的级别-在这种类别中，司机可以完全允许他们的车辆接管控制权。第三级仍然要求司机能够在系统请求时接手驾驶，而第四级和第五级则没有这样的要求。
特斯拉还在测试项目中推出了自己的车辆“全自动驾驶”功能，正在逐步扩展到更多的司机，但批评人士认为，尽管这个功能名为“全自动驾驶”，但它实际上并不是一个完全自动驾驶的系统，根据法规，它还没有被归类为完全自动驾驶系统。本田将于2021年3月推出Level 3 Legend，这将受到全球监管机构和普通司机的关注，作为对批量生产和监管机构批准的自动驾驶汽车系统的首批真正测试之一。
note_en:
Honda claims it will be the first automaker to mass-produce vehicles with autonomous capabilities that meet SAE Level 3 standards, with plans to begin producing and selling a version of its Honda Legend luxury sedan with fully approved automated driving equipment in Japan from next March. Honda announced the news via press release (via  Reuters) and this follows the approval by the Japanese government of the company’s ‘Traffic Jam Pilot’ autonomous tech, which for the first time will allow drivers to actually take their eyes off the road while it’s engaged.
 Honda’s Pro  Pilot   Assist is the feature that predates this forthcoming one, but it’s a Level 2 feature per the SAE scale, which means that while it can automatically control both speed and steering, drivers behind the wheel have to be constantly ready to take over manual control should the system require it. SAE Level 3 is the first that falls under a categorization that most experts feels qualifies as actually autonomous – wherein a driver can fully allow their vehicle to take over control. Level 3 still requires that a driver be able to take over driving when the system requests, while Levels 4 and 5 have no such requirement.
 Tesla   has also launched its own ‘full self-driving’ feature in its vehicles in a beta program that it’s expanding to more drivers gradually, but critics suggest that despite it’s name, it’s not actually a fully autonomous system, and it isn’t yet classified as such according to regulations. Honda’s launch of its Level 3 Legend in March 2021 will be one watched by regulators and ordinary drivers alike around the world as one of the first true tests of a mass-produced and regulator-approved autonomous vehicle system.
title: 本田将在3月前批量生产3级自动驾驶汽车
title_en: Honda to mass-produce Level 3 autonomous cars by March

note:
在选举之夜，29岁的哈桑·皮克(Hasan Piker)身穿海军蓝“伯尼2020”(Bernie 2020)运动衫，身穿“现在就有民主！”当他扑通一声坐在椅子上向他的数字观众发表讲话时，他戴上了棒球帽。
他说：“我告诉过你们一百次了，铁锈地带的地方有很多邮寄选票，他们不会立即统计。”“你现在看到的是不完整的数据！”接下来的话里夹杂着咒骂。
从那时起，以狂热的银幕形象著称的进步政治评论员皮克一直是Twitch上最受关注的流媒体用户。本周，他花了80多个小时坐在相机前，打开电脑上的大量标签，阅读新闻，为他的左倾千禧一代和Z世代的追随者提供分析。许多人说，他们觉得他坦率、略带混乱的风格比沉默寡言的有线新闻主播更容易让人产生共鸣。
“人们来找我是因为他们想听观点--也许也不是修剪整齐的观点，而是一个诚实的观点，”皮克说。
Twitch是一个以播放视频游戏而闻名的平台，近几个月来已经成为一个活跃的政治空间。今年夏天，活动人士和组织者播放了黑人生命也是为了种族平等而举行的游行和静坐活动。人们独白或主持讨论(通常是关于政治)的“只是聊天”流也变得越来越受欢迎。今年6月，《纽约时报》(New York Times)报道称，Twitch已经“变身为一个意想不到的社会激进主义中心”。
皮克出生于新泽西州，在土耳其长大，2013年毕业于罗格斯大学(Rutgers University)，主修传播和政治学两个专业。他为叔叔森克·维吾尔(Cenk Uygur)工作，后者是进步的在线新闻和评论节目“年轻的土耳其人”(The Young Turks)的创始人之一。
他最初为该项目做广告销售和业务开发，但最终想要做一些自己的事情。2016年，皮克提出了制作“崩溃”(The Breakdown)的想法，这是Facebook上的一部名为“年轻土耳其人”(Young Turks)的系列视频，旨在为左倾观众提供政治分析。事实证明，他对评论员托米·拉伦(Tomi Lahren)和特朗普总统移民禁令的尖锐批评很受欢迎。没过多久，派克就因为Facebook的常驻人物“唤醒BAE”而声名鹊起，他说自己对这个称谓很反感。
但到了2018年，皮克在Facebook上的回报逐渐减少，右翼新闻在该平台上的主导地位正在上升。他还注意到算法正在从视频转向。
同年3月，他创建了一个Twitch频道，并开始零星地播放视频。“我想要一个能让人们每天都聚集在一起的地方，”他说。
他的流媒体增长缓慢；第一个只有35名观众，但随着时间的推移，他开始参加其他流媒体的节目，并与其他内容创作者合作，这帮助扩大了他的受众。到了2019年，他几乎每天都在一次播放几个小时的流媒体。路上有些颠簸。皮克曾四次因违反该平台的版权和内容指导方针而被暂时封杀。
自始至终，皮克的目标都是改写他在新闻中看到的有关左翼的叙述。他认为，进步人士的形象存在问题，部分原因是新闻机构利用了对活动人士和组织者的不诚实描述。
他说：“在福克斯新闻(Fox News)的加速下，无论你在互联网上走到哪里，左翼都被视为歇斯底里、情绪化、蓝头发的社会正义斗士。”“他们把争取社会正义的观念变成了负面的东西。”
“‘SJW’被认为是一种贬义，”他补充道，他指的是“社会正义勇士”一词的缩写。“这对我来说太疯狂了。问题是，这些人有正义的事业，他们有权感情用事和沮丧，但不幸的是，右翼能够创造一个非常成功的故事。“。
今年1月，皮克过渡到全职流媒体工作。在此之前，当他仍然受雇于“年轻的土耳其人”时，他基本上是两班倒的工作，白天工作，一直工作到深夜和清晨。
民主党总统初选正在如火如荼地进行，派克在网上为数百万人报道和分析了初选过程。Twitch给他寄来了一个“IRL Backack”流媒体套件，让他可以进行现场直播，包括在内华达州组织活动和在波士顿举行伯尼·桑德斯(Bernie Sanders)集会。
今年夏天，当“黑人生命也是命”(Black Lives Matter)抗议活动开始时，派克也报道了这一点。他说：“我在某种程度上展示了当地民众的言论，而不是当地新闻或主流媒体的报道方式。”“我大体上批评了当地的新闻网络，它们过度关注抢劫，以及他们对这些抗议活动建立的所有其他比喻。”
在今年秋天的辩论中，皮克一直在他的频道上保持势头。10月20日，他与众议员亚历山德里亚·奥卡西奥-科尔特斯(Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez)和伊尔汗·奥马尔(Ilhan Omar)一起在我们中间玩游戏，吸引了数十万观众。他还开始为他的观众做好准备，他说这将是一个独一无二的选举之夜。
11月3日，皮克醒来，去了健身房，然后开始流泪--连续16个小时没有停下来。他的马拉松式选举之夜视频已经被观看了超过450万次，在巅峰时期有超过22.5万人同时观看。
让观众着迷的是皮克先生实时消费信息的方式。“昨晚，我看着皮克和他的客人打球，因为他在杂乱无章的浏览器上至少点击了三次错误的标签，”新闻周刊记者吉塔·杰克逊(Gita Jackson)上周在一篇文章中写道。“我看到了自己，也看到了我参与政治和新闻的方式，这不仅体现在派克的政治观点上，也体现在他使用互联网本身的方式上。”
33岁的纽约人安妮·亚历山大(Anne Alexander)在大选后的第二天看了几个小时皮克的频道。“哈桑以互联网的速度消费互联网，”她说。“他打开了5万个选项卡，从福克斯到CNN，再到推特，无所不包。”他正在从过道的另一边吸纳新闻。他在社交媒体上，在可靠的新闻来源上，他也会阅读所有收到的评论，有时他会听取评论员的意见，并对他们做出回应。这是非常有活力的。“。
Twitch首席运营官萨拉·克莱门斯(Sara Clemens)说，皮克的流媒体是过去一年Twitch如何将视频游戏以外的内容多元化的一个例子。她说，他的流媒体触及了Twitch的“核心”观众：千禧一代和Z世代。克莱门斯说，“这是吸引这些观众的一种非常有力的方式。”
尽管他经常接待主流新闻机构的记者，但皮克说，他并不想从事有线新闻事业。事实上，他说，在选举之夜，长达数小时的流媒体播放让他比有线电视新闻更有优势。
他说，对于过去一周长时间工作的专家和分析师来说，每天在镜头前工作10到11个小时是不寻常的。但对派克先生来说，这是常态。他说：“我每天都会流媒体播放这些时间。”
note_en:
On election night, Hasan Piker, 29, was dressed in a navy blue “Bernie 2020” sweatshirt and a “Democracy Now!” baseball hat when he plopped down in a chair to address his digital audience.
 “I told you guys like a hundred times that places in the Rust Belt have a lot of mail-in ballots they’re not counting immediately,” he said. “What you’re seeing right now is incomplete data!” The words that followed were peppered with expletives.
 Since then, Mr. Piker, a progressive political commentator known for his frenetic onscreen presence, has been the most-watched streamer on Twitch. He spent more than 80 hours this week in front of his camera, with tons of tabs open on his computer, reading out news and providing analysis for his left-leaning millennial and Gen Z followers. Many say they find his candid, slightly chaotic style more relatable than that of  buttoned-up cable news anchors.
  “People came to me because they wanted to hear a point of view — and maybe not a manicured point of view either, but an honest point of view,” Mr. Piker said.
 Twitch, a platform known for broadcasting video game play, has become a lively political space in recent months. This summer, activists and organizers streamed Black Lives Matter marches and sit-ins for racial equality. “Just chatting” streams, where people monologue or host discussions, oftentimes about politics, have also grown in popularity. In June, The New York Times  reported that Twitch has “transformed into an unexpected hub of social activism.”
  Born in New Jersey and raised in Turkey, Mr. Piker graduated from Rutgers University in 2013, with a double major in communications and political science, and took a job working for his uncle, Cenk Uygur, a founder of “The Young Turks,” a progressive online news and commentary program.
 He started off doing ad sales and business development for the program, but eventually wanted to make something of his own. In 2016, Mr. Piker pitched the idea for “The Breakdown,” a “Young Turks” video series on Facebook that would deliver political analysis for a left-leaning audience. His sharp criticisms of the commentator  Tomi Lahren and President Trump’s immigration ban proved to be a hit. Before long, Mr. Piker had gained fame as  Facebook's resident “woke bae,” a title he said he resents.
 But by 2018, Mr. Piker was experiencing diminishing returns on Facebook, and seeing the rise of  right-wing news  dominance on the platform.  He also noticed the algorithm shifting away from video.
  He set up a Twitch channel in March of that year and began streaming sporadically. “I wanted a place where I could have people congregate every day,” he said.
 His streams were slow growing; the first had just 35 viewers, but over time he began going on other streamers’ shows and collaborating with fellow content creators, which helped expand his audience. Come 2019, he was streaming for hours at a time, nearly every day. There were some bumps in the road. Mr. Piker has been banned temporarily four times for running afoul of the platform’s copyright and content guidelines.
 Throughout, Mr. Piker’s goal has been to rewrite the narrative he sees in the news about the left. He felt that progressives had an image problem in part because news organizations were playing into bad-faith portrayals of activists and organizers.
  “Everywhere you went on the internet, accelerated by Fox News, the left was seen as hysterical, emotional, blue-haired social justice warriors,” he said. “They turned the concept of fighting for social justice into something negative.”
 “‘SJW’ is considered a pejorative,” he added, referring to the abbreviation of the term “social justice warrior.” “That’s crazy to me. The thing is, those people have a righteous cause, they have the right to be emotional and frustrated, but unfortunately, the right has been able to create a very successful narrative.”
 In January, Mr. Piker transitioned to streaming full time. Previously, while still employed by “The Young Turks,” he had been essentially working double shifts, working during the day and streaming well into the night and early morning.
  The Democratic presidential primary was in full swing, and Mr. Piker covered and analyzed the process for millions online. Twitch sent him an “ IRL Backpack” streaming kit that allowed him to broadcast on-the-ground, including from organizing events in Nevada and a Bernie Sanders rally in Boston.
 When the Black Lives Matter protests took hold this summer, Mr. Piker covered that too. “I showed what the people on the ground were saying rather than the way local news or the mainstream media was covering it to some degree,” he said. “I broadly criticized the local news networks that hyper-focused on looting and all these other tropes they were building about these protests.”
 Mr. Piker kept the momentum up on his channel throughout the debates this fall. On Oct. 20, he played the game  Among Us with Representatives Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez and Ilhan Omar for an audience of hundreds of thousands. He also began to prepare his viewers for what he said would be an election night like no other.
  On Nov. 3, Mr. Piker woke up, hit the gym, then began to stream — and didn’t stop for 16 hours. His marathon  election-night stream has been viewed more than 4.5 million times and had more than 225,000 concurrent viewers at its peak.
 What viewers find captivating is the way Mr. Piker consumes information in real time. “Last night I watched Piker and his guests play for time as he clicked on the wrong tab in his disorganized browser at least three times,” Gita Jackson, a reporter for  Vice, wrote in an article last week. “I saw myself, and the way that I engage with politics and the news, in not just Piker’s political opinions but the way he uses the internet itself.”
  Anne Alexander, a 33-year-old New Yorker, watched Mr. Piker’s channel for hours the day after the election. “Hasan consumes the internet at the speed of the internet,” she said. “He has 50,000 tabs open and he’s going from Fox to CNN to Twitter to whatever. He’s consuming news from across the aisle. He’s on social media, on reputable news sources, he’s also reading all the comments that come in, then sometimes he’s listening to commentators and responding to them. It’s extremely dynamic.”
 Sara Clemens, Twitch’s chief operating officer, said Mr. Piker’s stream was one example of how Twitch has diversified its content beyond video games over the past year. She said his stream reached Twitch’s “core” viewership: millennials and Gen Z.“It’s a really powerful way to engage with that audience,” Ms. Clemens said.
 Though he frequently hosts journalists from mainstream news outlets, Mr. Piker said he doesn’t have a desire to pursue a career in cable news. In fact, he said that being so comfortable streaming for hours gave him an advantage over cable news on election night.
 For the pundits and analysts working long hours this past week, he said, 10-and 11-hour days in front of the camera are unusual. But for Mr. Piker, it’s the norm. “I stream those hours every single day,” he said.
title: 哈桑·派克(Hasan Piker)的简介，他是一名进步的Twitch流媒体用户，在选举之夜的高峰期拥有22.5万+并发观众，使他成为Twitch收视率最高的流媒体用户
title_en: Profile of Hasan Piker, a progressive Twitch streamer who had 225K+ concurrent viewers at the peak on election night, making him Twitch's most-watched streamer
